coredata.dc:title: White light emission from chiral carbonized polymer dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Jiawei
Li, Dexin
Wang, Xinnan
Li, Jie
Ba, Xinwu
Wang, Sujuan
coredata.dc:description: A series of novel carbonized polymer dots have been synthesized and characterized via the condensation reaction between curcumin and various amino acids with the assistance of microwave. More strikingly, both CPDs prepared from curcumin and L/D-aspartic acid can emit strong white light in aqueous solution and in doped polyvinylpyrrolidone. The white light emitting diodes based on L-Asp-CPD/PVP and D-Asp-CPD/PVP exhibit the CIE coordinates of (0.36, 0.34) and (0.38, 0.32) at the drive current of 0.12 A, CRI of 84.9 and 89.7, and CCT of 4385 K and 3410 K, respectively. The stability of LED derived from D-Asp-CPD was better than that from L-Asp-CPD at operational time of 5 h and the driven current of 0.02 A.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Chiral
Light-emitting diodes
White light
Curcumin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Chemical structures of curcumin and various amino acids. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of L-Asp-CPD and D-Asp-CPD in aqueous solution. (c) CD spectra of Asp and Asp-CPD in aqueous solution. Fluorescence spectra of L-Asp-CPD (d) and D-Asp-CPD (e), and insets are the fluorescence images excited at 365 nm. UV–vis absorption (f) and fluorescence (g) spectra of L-Ser-CPD, L-Glu-CPD, L-Arg-CPD and L-Pro-CPD in aqueous solution.
HR-TEM images and size distributions of L-Asp-CPD (a)/(c) and D-Asp-CPD (b)/(d). (e) XRD patterns of L-Asp-CPD and D-Asp-CPD. (f) FT-IR spectra of curcumin, L-Asp, D-Asp, L-Asp-CPD, D-Asp-CPD.
Photographs of L-Asp-CPD/PVP (a) and D-Asp-CPD/PVP (b) composites with different mass ratio under daylight and 365 nm UV light. The fluorescence spectra of Asp-CPD/PVP with different ratio (c)/(d).

The device structures and photographs of L-Asp-CPD (a) and D-Asp-CPD (b), Fluorescence emission spectra of WLED devices based on L-Asp-CPD (c) and D-Asp-CPD (d) at 0.02A, 0.04 A, 0.06A, 0.08 A, 0.1 A and 0.12 A. The CCT and CRI of WLEDs based on L-Asp-CPD (e) and D-Asp-CPD (f) at different currents. CIE chromaticity coordinates of the WLEDs using L-Asp-CPD (g) and D-Asp-CPD (h) as the phosphor.

head.ce:title: White light emission from chiral carbonized polymer dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University, Baoding, 071002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University, Baoding, 071002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of novel carbonized polymer dots have been synthesized and characterized via the condensation reaction between curcumin and various amino acids with the assistance of microwave. More strikingly, both CPDs prepared from curcumin and L/D-aspartic acid can emit strong white light in aqueous solution and in doped polyvinylpyrrolidone. The white light emitting diodes based on L-Asp-CPD/PVP and D-Asp-CPD/PVP exhibit the CIE coordinates of (0.36, 0.34) and (0.38, 0.32) at the drive current of 0.12 A, CRI of 84.9 and 89.7, and CCT of 4385 K and 3410 K, respectively. The stability of LED derived from D-Asp-CPD was better than that from L-Asp-CPD at operational time of 5 h and the driven current of 0.02 A.

Carbonized polymer dots have garnered considerable attention owing to their excellent optical properties. This study provides a facile strategy to prepare novel carbonized polymer dots from curcumin and aspartic acid. The prepared carbonized polymer dots can emit strong white light in solid state after mixing with PVP. They are also successfully used to fabricate a white light-emitting diode.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting materials are of great importance because they have emerged as promising candidates for next-generation solid-state lighting sources in flat panel displays and light emitting diodes (LEDs) [1–3]. Undoubted, the white LEDs offer the major light sources in the household and industrial lighting markets nowadays. Most white LEDs are achieved by combining blue light emitter with orange counterpart or with green/red counterparts that can covering the complete visible area until now [4–6]. While, such devices usually encounter some detracting properties including phase separation, poor stability and self-absorption. In comparison, single-component white light emitters can effectively avoid these weakness, which boost the device performance to a great extent [7–12]. All the results drive us to acquire white light from single emitter via rational design and optimization of functionalized materials. It is widely known that carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), as a new class of luminescent carbon dots, usually possess fascinating properties including high chemical stability, cost effectiveness, low toxicity, excellent optical properties and easy functionalization, making them easy use in various aspects [13–16]. In addition, they can be obtained from various low-cost carbon sources such as fruits and vegetables et al., leading to promoting the wide application in bio-fields and electric devices [17–19]. Thanks for their favorable fluorescent properties, CPDs can be utilized as powerful alternatives for LED devices [20]. While, most of them emit single fluorescence band, and expanding the range covering the visible region and examining their application are urgent. For example, Meng and co-workers described the preparation of single-component white light emissive CPDs for urea and phosphoric acid in aqueous solution, and such gram-scale CPDs showed the quantum yield of up to 41 % in the solid state [21]. Lu et al. also constructed CPDs by using a single unconjugated glucose through a classical hydrothermal reaction [22]. More interestingly, the LED device based on the resulting CPDs with the emission wavelength covering the entire visible region emitted white light with the CIE color coordinates of (0.34, 0.36). As we know, chiral compounds are plentiful and crucial in nature. Moreover, the chiral materials underpin their function in LED electronics and biological systems [23–25]. In the traditional LED device, visual performance may be damaged by glare emitted at high intensity. The elimination of glare will play a crucial role in the development of LED. Chiral materials with circularly polarized luminescence properties may provide a resolution to eliminate the utilization of antiglare filters [26]. Such chirality-dependent investigation benefit heavily from the success in approaching single-chirality molecules. Thus, integrating CPDs with chiral elements can serve as an important platform to further expand the dimensionality of chiroptical signals and tune light-matter interactions. To date, creating CPDs-based chiral white light-emitting devices remains elusive [27].

In this work, a family of fluorescent CPDs named as Asp-CPD, Ser-CPD, Glu-CPD, Arg-CPD, Pro-CPD have been successfully synthesized using curcumin and various amino acids including aspartic acid, serine, glutamic acid, arginine and proline as the precursors via simple one-step way with the assistance of microwave oven (Fig. 1 a), and characterized through Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscope (TEM), UV–vis absorption, fluorescence and circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. Unexpectedly, the as-formed CPD prepared from curcumin and L/D-aspartic acid emit white light in aqueous solution and in polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP)-doped composites. The white light emitting diodes based on L-Asp-CPD/PVP and D-Asp-CPD/PVP exhibit the CIE coordinates of (0.36, 0.34) and (0.38, 0.32) at the drive current of 0.12 A, CRI of 84.9 and 89.7, and CCT of 4385 K and 3410 K, respectively. The stability of LED derived from D-Asp-CPD was better than that from L-Asp-CPD at operational time of 5 h and the driven current of 0.02 A.
The CPDs were synthesized using curcumin and various amino acids as carbon source through one-step microwave assisted reaction as follows.

L-Asp-CPD: A mixture of aspartic acid (2 g)/1,3-diaminopropane (1 mL) in ultrapure water (15 mL) and curcumin (24 mg) in dichloromethane (12 mL) was mixed and sonicated for 15 min. Then the solution was stirred for 210 s in a microwave oven (TOSHIBA ER-SS20CNW) operating at a power of 560 W. The mixture solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min. The filtrate was then purified by dialyzing against deionized water using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off = 500–1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the unreacted substance. L-Asp-CPD was obtained by freeze-drying the filtrate.

L-Ser-CPD: A mixture of serine (1.576 g)/1,3-diaminopropane (1 mL) in ultrapure water (15 mL) and curcumin (24 mg) in dichloromethane (12 mL) was mixed and sonicated for 15 min. Then the solution was stirred for 210 s in a microwave oven operating at a power of 560 W. The mixture solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min. The filtrate was then purified by dialyzing against deionized water using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off = 500–1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the unreacted substance. L-Ser-CPD was obtained by freeze-drying the filtrate.

L-Glu-CPD: A mixture of glutamic acid (2.2 g)/1,3-diaminopropane (1 mL) in ultrapure water (15 mL) and curcumin (24 mg) in dichloromethane (12 mL) was mixed and sonicated for 15 min. Then the solution was stirred for 210 s in a microwave oven operating at a power of 560 W. The mixture solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min. The filtrate was then purified by dialyzing against deionized water using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off = 500–1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the unreacted substance. L-Glu-CPD was obtained by freeze-drying the filtrate.

L-Arg-CPD: A mixture of arginine (2.63 g)/1,3-diaminopropane (1 mL) in ultrapure water (15 mL) and curcumin (24 mg) in dichloromethane (12 mL) was mixed and sonicated for 15 min. Then the solution was stirred for 210 s in a microwave oven operating at a power of 560 W. The mixture solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min. The filtrate was then purified by dialyzing against deionized water using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off = 500–1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the unreacted substance. L-Arg-CPD was obtained by freeze-drying the filtrate.

L-Pro-CPD: A mixture of proline (1.727 g)/1,3-diaminopropane (1 mL) in ultrapure water (15 mL) and curcumin (24 mg) in dichloromethane (12 mL) was mixed and sonicated for 15 min. Then the solution was stirred for 210 s in a microwave oven operating at a power of 560 W. The mixture solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min. The filtrate was then purified by dialyzing against deionized water using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off = 500–1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the unreacted substance. L-Pro-CPD was obtained by freeze-drying the filtrate.

The size and morphology were measured using the transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA and JEOL JEM2100). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were tested on an XRD spectrometer (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10, Thermo, USA) equipped with a deuterated triglycine sulfate detector was used to characterize the surface functional groups. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) were performed on Scientific ESCALAB QXi. UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra were performed on UV-3600 (Shimadzu, Japan) and F-7000 (HITACHI, Japan), respectively.

Different dosage of CPDs and PVP with the mass ratios of CPDs to PVP of 1:400, 1:200, 1:100, 1:40 were dissolved in deionized water and sonicated for 10 min. Afterwards, the solutions were dropped onto the watch glass. After drying in an oven at 60 °C, the resulting solid films were ground into powders in an agate mortar to obtain well-dispersed CPDs/PVP composite.

The CPDs/PVP composites with mass ratio of 1:200 were chosen as active layer to fabricate WLED devices. The CPDs/PVP composites were firstly mixed with the UV curing adhesive and the mass ratio was 1:1. After that, the mixture was coated onto the blue-lighting-emitting LED chips (λ = 450 nm, San'an Optoelectronics). The WLED device was obtained by curing process for 20 s under UV irradiation and was measured on the OHSP-350 M LED Fast-Scan Spectrophotometer (Hopoo Light & Color Technology, China). The spectra from 380 nm to 850 nm were collected with emission wavelength of 440 nm.

The CPDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted one-step method using curcumin and various amino acids as precursors, and the details for the synthetic procedures were described as in the section of Experimental Methods. The optical properties were firstly examined in aqueous solution through UV–vis absorption, fluorescence and circular dichroism spectra. As seen in Fig. 1b, both the desired L-Asp-CPD and D-Asp-CPD exhibited similar absorption profile with 347 nm together with broad-band, being assigned to the n-π* and π-π* transitions of π-conjugated system [28]. In addition, L-Asp and D-Asp displayed mirror symmetry circular dichroism spectra with maximum signals centered at 197 nm. While, the resulting L-Asp-CPD and D-Asp-CPD exhibited obvious CD response, different from their corresponding precursors L-Asp and D-Asp (Fig. 1c). More interestingly, they could emit strong white light and the broad-band spectra covered the entire visible region excited at 330, 350, 370, 390 nm (Fig. 1d and e). In comparison, other CPDs prepared from the similar operation condition compared with L-Asp-CPD presented strong emission in aqueous solution at 512 nm for L-Ser-CPD, 390 nm for L-Glu-CPD, 406 nm for L-Asp-CPD, 464 nm for L-Pro-CPD, falling into green, blue, blue and cyan-emission region, respectively (Fig. 1f and g). Such findings implied that the amino acids were pivotal precursors that could affect the optical properties of the desired CPDs to a significant extent. The bandgap energy of L-Asp-CPD could be calculated from UV–vis absorption spectra to be the lowest value among these five CPDs (Fig. S1), which demonstrated the presence of long wavelength emission [29,30]. Therefore, the fluorescence emission spectra of Asp-CPD covered the entire visible region and could emit white light.

In addition, L-Asp-CPD-1 and L-Asp-CPD-2 were further synthesized under the same condition using feeding ratio of amino acids to curcumin 130:1 and 330:1, respectively. Both L-Asp-CPD-1 and L-Asp-CPD-2 could emit white fluorescence and the fluorescence spectra were shown in Fig. S2. It could be concluded that the fluorescence emission spectra were not obviously changed with the changing of feeding ratio of amino acids to curcumin. However, L-Asp-CPD with feeding ratio of 230:1 demonstrated the highest fluorescence quantum yield. Therefore, the feeding ratio of 230:1 was utilized in the following experiments.

Thereafter, the as-formed Asp-CPD were characterized by HR-TEM, FT-IR, X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in detail. HR-TEM images suggested that L-Asp-CPD and D-Asp-CPD were well dispersed and the spherical shapes with the average size of 8.2 nm and 6.8 nm were observed (Fig. 2 a-d), respectively. Meanwhile, the insets of HR-TEM images showed that both corresponding interlayer spacing for L-Asp-CPD and D-Asp-CPD was 0.21 nm, which was labelled by arrows. XRD patterns displayed a broad peak centered at 2θ of 21.01° (Fig. 2e), corresponding the 002 plane of graphite carbon [19,31]. Both of L-Asp-CPD and D-Asp-CPD possessed the bands at 3431 cm−1 (the stretching vibrations of O–H), 3228 cm−1 (the stretching vibrations of N–H), 2946 cm−1 (symmetric stretching vibrations of C–H), 1701 cm−1(stretching vibrations of the CO bond) (Fig. 2f). The blue-shifted peaks of L-Asp-CPD/D-Asp-CPD at 1701 cm−1 compared with curcumin (1630 cm−1) and L-Asp/D-Asp (1685 cm−1) manifested that curcumin could react with Asp at elevated temperature.

Furthermore, XPS was carried out to realize the chemical composition and end functional groups of the desired CPDs (Fig. S3). The XPS full scan results showed the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen (Figs. S3a and S3e). The C1s spectra were formed into three peaks including 284.8, 288.18 and 291.33 eV, belonging correspondingly to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N and CO (Figs. S3b and S3f). Meanwhile, The N1s spectra indicated that the three peaks at 399.33, 401.19 and 403.19 eV were assigned to pyrrole, graphene and nitrogen oxide, respectively (Figs. S3c and S3g) [32]. In addition, the O1s spectra mainly contained two signals at 531.16 eV (CO) and 534.54 eV (C–O) [33]. The above-mentioned analyses provided a support that L-Asp/D-Asp and curcumin were initially reacted together to generate branched polymers, which were further carbonized to produce the CPDs with carbon-core and COOH, NH2 groups on their periphery.

The effect of various parameters such as ionic strength, solution concentration, pH, temperature on the stability of the desired CPDs was examined as shown in Fig. S4. There was almost negligible change for the emission intensity upon addition of Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mn2+, implying a good enough stability (Figs. S4a and S4e). In comparison, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Sn4+ were injected into the CPDs solution, obvious fluorescence quenching occurred. However, the fluorescence was completely quenched upon addition of electron deficient Fe3+, which might be ascribed to its strong affinity towards electron rich groups including –OH, –NH2, –OR in the surface of CPDs. The fluorescence intensities of L-Asp-CPD and D-Asp-CPD exhibit slight changed along with the NaCl concentration increasing from 0 to 1 M, further ascertaining their stability (Figs. S4b and S4f). The fluorescence intensities of L-Asp-CPD and D-Asp-CPD remained relatively unchangeable within the pH range of 3–11. However, obvious decrease was found with the pH values of 1 and 13, which might be caused by the protonation and deprotonation process (Figs. S4c and S4g). The fluorescence intensity exhibited slight change after continuous rising in temperature from 20 °C to 100 °C, affirming the thermal stability (Figs. S4d and S4h).

The as-prepared CPDs displayed distinct white light in aqueous solution (Fig. 1d and e) upon irradiation at 365 nm, but very weak fluorescence was observed in the solid state (Fig. 3 a and b), which might be attributed to strong aggregation. To suppress the aggregation and expand their potential application in organic devices, such materials were doped into PVP, where all the Asp-CPD/PVP composites bearing different mass ratio of 1:400, 1:200, 1:100 and 1:40 could emit white light (Fig. 3a and b). The fluorescence spectra also showcased that Asp-CPD/PVP with mass ratio of 1:200 displayed highest intensity (Fig. 3c and d). The fluorescence quantum yield of L-Asp-CPD and L-Asp-CPD/PVP with the ratio of 1:200 were calculated to be 2.2 % and 4.0 % respectively, by using an integrating sphere, further manifesting that PVP could decrease the aggregation and attenuate the fluorescence quenching to a large extent.

With these materials in hand, the WLED devices were fabricated by using the Asp-CPD/PVP composites and blue-light-emitting LED chips with the emission peak centering at 450 nm by curing process for 20s under UV irradiation, where the solid Asp-CPD/PVP composites were directly coated on the surface of UV-LED chip (Fig. 4 a and b). The photos of LEDs built by using Asp-CPD/PVP power were shown in Fig. 4a and b, which emitted bright white light during operation. The fluorescence emission spectra of the WLED lamp contained two emission bands, belonging to the emission of the UV-LED chip (434 nm) and L-Asp-CPD (590 nm)/D-Asp-CPD (618 nm), respectively (Fig. 4c and d). In addition, the shapes of the fluorescence emission spectra remained unchanged and the intensity enhanced along with the current increasing from 0.02 A to 0.04 A, 0.06 A, 0.08 A, 0.10 A, then to 0.12 A, indicating a good stability of the WLED devices. In addition, the CCT values presented a slight increase upon the increase of the driven current, but there were no noticeable changes for CRI under the same operation condition. The CCT and CRI approached to 4385K and 84.9 for L-Asp-CPD-based device, and 3410K and 89.7 for D-Asp-CPD-based on device with the driven current of 0.12 A (Fig. 4e and f), highlighting the excellent stability of the white light generated from the WLED lamp. The CIE color coordinates were located at (0.36, 0.34) for L-Asp-CPD-based device and (0.38, 0.32) for D-Asp-CPD-based device at the drive current of 0.12 A, being close to that of pure white light (0.33, 0.33). Meanwhile, the luminous efficiency were 2.414 lm/W and 3.174 lm/W (Fig. 4g/4h and Table S1/S2). The intensity stability of LED was measured at 5 h of operational time and the driven current of 0.02 A (Fig. S5). It could be found that the stability of D-LED was better than that of L-LED. In general, the white LED with CRI >75 and CCT <4500 K usually emited warm white light, which boosted the practical application in indoor lighting, especial in hospital operating room. Therefore, such white LEDs based on Asp-CPD meet the commercial standard. Besides that, the CPD synthesized from biomass were environmental friendly, leading to the reduction in production cost and avoiding the use of noble metals.

In summary, we have designed and synthesized five novel gram-scale CPDs and the synthesis was finished on a one-step process involving condensation reaction between curcumin and various amino acids with the help of microwave. Among them, the Asp-CPD with different chiral signal both exhibited white light serendipitously in aqueous solution and in the doped PVP solid state. The LEDs were fabricated by combining L-Asp-CPD/PVP or D-Asp-CPD/PVP as a single emitter with a UV chip, which emit warm white light with CRI changing from 83.8 to 89.7 and CCT changing from 2938K to 4385K. The stability of D-LED at 5 h of operational time and the driven current of 0.02 A was better than that of L-LED. In a word, our systematic study might provide a platform for directly preparing chiral CPDs from biomass, which can simplify the production procedure, avoid the use of noble metals, and boost the practical application in LED device.

Jiawei Dong: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Dexin Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xinnan Wang: Validation, Investigation. Jie Li: Methodology, Data curation. Xinwu Ba: Supervision, Resources. Sujuan Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Key Project of the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (B2021201043), Advanced Talents Incubation Program of Hebei University (521000981308) and Post-graduate's Innovation Fund Project of Hebei University (HBU2023SS012).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Management of singlet and triplet excitons for efficient white organic light-emitting devices
Nature
